entry.title: The Atlantic: 22 Jun, 2022
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:
  • The Story of Fred, a Mastodon
  • How to Be a Good Person Without Annoying Everyone
  • Side Effects May Include … A Completely New Hair Color?
  • Black Flamingo
  • What My Mom Told Me About America Before Roe
  • Welcome to the Summer of Dancing and Darkness
  • Farewell to Hong Kong and Its Big Lie
  • What Are Trump Supporters So Afraid Of?

  • The Most American Form of Architecture Isn’t Going Anywhere
  • Squirrels Could Make Monkeypox a Forever Problem
  • Dave Chappelle’s Not Kidding
  • A Substitution
  • Saïd Sayrafiezadeh on the Performance of Fiction
  • Cancel Zoning

  • How a Google Employee Fell for the Eliza Effect

  • China’s ‘Very Dangerous Trajectory’
  • Beware the Luxury Beach Resort

  • Chris Hemsworth Finds His Villainous Niche

  • Perfume Genius Sings the Body Electric
  • The Book That Captures My Life as a Dad
  • A Lost Trove of Civil War Gold, an FBI Excavation, and Some Very Angry Treasure Hunters
  • The Book That Never Stops Changing
  • It’s Time for Pixar to Move on From Toy Story
  • Barry Isn’t a Comedy Anymore. But It’s Become an Even Better Show.
  • Britain’s Unbridgeable Divide
  • Where Russia’s Declinist Rage Isn’t Enough
  • Photos of the Week: Paddle Steamer, Floating Restaurant, Glass Bridge
  • Google’s AI Is Something Even Stranger Than Conscious
  • The Personal Brand Is Dead
  • Photos: Devastating Floods Hit the Yellowstone Region
  • Will These Be the Last Polar Bears on Earth?
  • A Hotter, Poorer, and Less Free America
  • A Frog So Small, It Could Not Frog
  • An Unusual Movie Pick for Father’s Day
  • 10 Hours, Two Theme Parties
  • The Awkward Intimacy of Therapy
  • Trump’s Dangerous Wannabes
  • Two Battles for Democracy
  • There May Be a Blunt-Force Fix for Inflation
News and editorial about politics, culture, entertainment, tech, etc. Contains many articles not seen in The Atlantic magazine

News, Analysis and Opinion from POLITICO

The Diplomat is a current-affairs magazine for the Asia-Pacific, with news and analysis on politics, security, business, technology and life across the region.

entry.title: Nature: 23 Jun, 2022
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:
  • Equity must be baked into randomized controlled trials
  • I have funds to buy reagents, but not remedies
  • How COVID has deepened inequality — in six stark graphics
  • These experiments could lift millions out of dire poverty
  • Tackling inequality takes social reform
  • Not all inequalities are alike
  • The rise of inequality research: can spanning disciplines help tackle injustice?
  • Shark researcher attacks lack of diversity in marine science
  • UK’s rupture with Horizon Europe is totally unnecessary
  • Origami mini-robot does gymnastics for a good cause
  • Microplastics sully Antarctic snow — and science might be to blame
  • What’s in that tea? DNA reveals footprints of bugs that went before
  • Fred the mastodon’s tusk tells of his restless travels
  • Vast tropical tree farms push into biodiversity hotspots
  • Near absolute zero is achieved for a new group of elements
  • Human brains run hot — sometimes more than 40 °C
  • Milky Way mega-map and UK science turmoil
  • COVID and smell loss: answers begin to emerge
  • Max Planck’s cherished autonomy questioned following criticism of misconduct investigations
  • Legendary bacterial evolution experiment enters new era
  • Ancient DNA traces origin of Black Death
  • African researchers lead campaign for equity in global collaborations
  • New COVID drugs face delays as trials grow more difficult
  • Bernard Bigot (1950–2022)
  • Sustainable small-scale fisheries can help people and the planet
  • IPCC decolonization call — policymakers must listen
  • Nature-based solutions for global climate adaptation
  • Misinformation: broaden definition to curb its societal influence
  • Could artificial intelligence hijack author contributions?
  • Exercise molecule burns away hunger
  • Collisions hint that four neutrons form a transient isolated entity
  • Mutations matter even if proteins stay the same
  • From the archive: respect for reptiles, and an astronomical centenary
  • 60 years of chemistry of the noble gases
  • Crystalline order offers access to high speeds for organic transistors
  • Two-dimensional materials prospects for non-volatile spintronic memories
  • Solar flare accelerates nearly all electrons in a large coronal volume
  • Observation of a correlated free four-neutron system
  • Continuous Bose–Einstein condensation
  • Many-body theory of positron binding to polyatomic molecules
  • Engineering topological states in atom-based semiconductor quantum dots
  • Organic bipolar transistors
  • Asymmetric pore windows in MOF membranes for natural gas valorization
  • Hadean isotopic fractionation of xenon retained in deep silicates
  • The source of the Black Death in fourteenth-century central Eurasia
  • Synonymous mutations in representative yeast genes are mostly strongly non-neutral
  • Spatiotemporal dynamics of noradrenaline during learned behaviour
  • Molecularly defined circuits for cardiovascular and cardiopulmonary control
  • Lifelong multilineage contribution by embryonic-born blood progenitors
  • Discovery of bioactive microbial gene products in inflammatory bowel disease
  • The role of NSP6 in the biogenesis of the SARS-CoV-2 replication organelle
  • Pregnancy enables antibody protection against intracellular infection
  • Targeting SLC7A11 improves efferocytosis by dendritic cells and wound healing in diabetes
  • An exercise-inducible metabolite that suppresses feeding and obesity
  • Androgen receptor activity in T cells limits checkpoint blockade efficacy
  • Androgen receptor blockade promotes response to BRAF/MEK-targeted therapy
  • Mitochondrial base editor induces substantial nuclear off-target mutations
  • Cohesin-mediated loop anchors confine the locations of human replication origins
  • Structural basis of GABA reuptake inhibition
  • Landfalling hurricane track modes and decay
  • Reply to: Landfalling hurricane track modes and decay
  • Author Correction: Defining the consequences of genetic variation on a proteome-wide scale
  • Publisher Correction: Virtual communication curbs creative idea generation
  • Publisher Correction: Genomic reconstruction of the SARS CoV-2 epidemic in England
  • Smell
Nature is a weekly international multidisciplinary scientific journal publishing peer-reviewed research in all fields of science and technology on the basis of its originality, importance, interdisciplinary interest, timeliness, accessibility, elegance and surprising conclusions. Nature also provides rapid, authoritative, insightful and arresting news and interpretation of topical and coming trends affecting science, scientists and the wider public.

  • Advice to Startup Founders: Prepare to Fail

  • The Morbid War Over Online Obituaries
  • The Wave-Conquering, Metaverse-Crashing Life of Kai Lenny
  • Welcome to Miami, Where All Your Memes Come True!
Wired is a full-color monthly American magazine, published in both print and online editions, that reports on how emerging technologies affect culture, the economy and politics. Monthly edition, best run at the start of every month.

entry.title: Nautilus: 15 Jun, 2022
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:
  • This Planetary Scientist Is Always Reaching for Something Big
  • Gravitational Waves Continue to Astound
  • Were It Not for Cosmic Good Fortune, We Wouldn’t Be Here
  • Mysteries Are to Be Embraced, But Also to Be Solved
  • How Much Is the Ocean Worth?
  • Reshuffled Rivers Bolster the Amazon’s Hyper-Biodiversity
  • Who Are the Scientists Here?
  • The Race to Explore the Ocean’s Twilight Zone
  • The Oceans Are Teeming with Unknown Species
  • The Giraffe Neck Evolved for Sexual Combat
  • Life’s First Peptides May Have Grown on RNA Strands
  • You Eat a Credit Card’s Worth of Plastic Every Week
  • Unlocking Mom’s Brain
  • No Two Human Brains Are Alike
  • Portrait of the Human as a Young Hominin
  • Thinking Like a Scientist Will Make You Happier
  • What Is Time?
  • Doctor Strange and the Multiverse in Science
  • The Secrets of the Blind Salamander
Nautilus is a different kind of science magazine. Our stories take you into the depths of science and spotlight its ripples in our lives and cultures. We believe any subject in science, no matter how complex, can be explained with clarity and vitality.

entry.title: The Third Pole: 14 Jun, 2022

entry.title: TIME: June 20th, 2022 | Vol. 199, No. 23 | U.S.

Founded in Chicago by Harriet Monroe in 1912, Poetry is the oldest monthly devoted to verse in the English-speaking world.
